Alright. For the next fifteen minutes we're walking through a 2021 review by Zhao, Blaabjerg, and Wang out of Aalborg — their map of how AI has swept through power electronics over the last decade. I'll lay out their taxonomy, go phase by phase with real examples, and then tell you the three things I think they got wrong. Or at least, under-weighted.

Quick pitch on why this matters. Everything we've covered in this course — smart grid, grid-edge, EV charging, solar, data-center PSUs — all of it runs on power-electronic converters. And the converters are getting harder. Wide-bandgap devices like SiC and GaN are pushing switching frequencies a full order of magnitude past silicon IGBTs, while the grid side demands tighter integration and more telemetry. Classical methods — hand-tuned PI, first-principles design loops — are hitting ceilings. The paper's thesis is simple: AI isn't the research curiosity it was in 2005. It's now deployed across the full PE lifecycle — design, control, and maintenance. Here's their map.

Forty seconds on the paper itself. It's a review — not a new algorithm, a map. Five hundred plus publications surveyed and bucketed into a lifecycle-by-method taxonomy. CORPE, which Blaabjerg and Wang run at Aalborg, is the most-cited PE reliability group in the world, so this carries weight. The structure I'm using: walk the taxonomy, go deep on each phase with real examples, then give my critical take on what the paper doesn't say.

Here's the spine. Three rows — design, control, maintenance. Four columns — expert systems, fuzzy logic, metaheuristics, machine learning. The striking thing is the publication share on the right. Of five hundred papers, control is seventy-eight percent. Design is ten. Maintenance is twelve. Control dominates because the problem is clean — real-time sensor data, a well-defined plant, and tracking error as a crisp objective. Design and maintenance are harder to formulate as AI tasks, which is exactly why they're under-represented, and I'd argue exactly where the open research opportunity sits. Let me take each toolkit in turn.

Four tools, four regimes. Expert systems — the oldest — encode a senior engineer's if-then rules. Explainability is their superpower; the weakness is you can't codify judgment that the engineer can't articulate. Fuzzy logic was the breakthrough of the eighties and nineties — it lets you reason about "somewhat high current" as a first-class variable, and it's still quietly shipping in a lot of motor drives today. Metaheuristics — GA, PSO, simulated annealing — are gradient-free. That's the whole game for design, where the cost surface is multi-objective, non-convex, and switches topology halfway through a search. And machine learning is the fastest-growing category — SVM, CNN, LSTM, RL, you name it. The paper is refreshingly sober here: no universal winner. Each tool fits a regime.

Design is only ten percent of the literature, but it's where metaheuristics shine. Take the problem: specify a DC-DC converter for a three-fifty-kilowatt EV charger. You have buck, boost, interleaved, resonant, multi-level topologies on the shelf — each with its own magnetics, caps, semiconductors. The design space is combinatorial. Classical flow is an engineer iterating analytically — maybe ten options in a working day. AI-augmented flow lets a GA or PSO explore thousands of candidates in simulation per hour. No gradients needed — critical, because the cost surface is non-differentiable when you're jumping between topologies. The output isn't a winner; it's a Pareto front. The engineer picks a point on the frontier. The newer move the paper flags is DNN surrogates — train a neural net on a simulation corpus and use it as a fast approximator inside the search loop. SPICE evaluations drop from seconds to microseconds. The framing shift: AI isn't replacing the engineer here. It's the assistant that explores the space the engineer can't cover by hand.

Control is the heart of the field — seventy-eight percent of the papers live here, and it's not just volume, the work is genuinely mature. Let me walk up the stack. Bottom tier: fuzzy controllers. Not exotic — they've been shipping in motor drives and DC-DC converters for thirty years. They handle sensor noise and nonlinearities like saturation and deadtime more gracefully than rigid PI gains. Middle tier: neural-net controllers. Rather than hand-tune, you let a NN learn the plant's inverse from input-output data. Modern MPC is expensive — it's solving an optimization at every sample step — so NN approximations of MPC policies give you near-MPC performance at microsecond inference times. That's the unlock for converter-scale switching frequencies. Top tier, research frontier: reinforcement learning. The pitch is multi-objective optimization in one shot — efficiency, THD, thermal, device lifetime, all traded off by the RL agent. The unsolved piece, which I'll come back to, is certification: how do you convince an IEC functional-safety auditor that an RL policy on a grid-connected inverter won't do something unsafe? Nobody has a good answer yet.

Maintenance is where ML has the cleanest value proposition — and, I'll just say it up front, where I think the next five years actually matter. Here's why cleanest. Classical condition monitoring is model-based. You write a physics model for IGBT degradation — bond-wire fatigue, solder-layer cracking — and detect deviations. Those models are conservative and hard to calibrate. The data-driven approach inverts it: instrument a fleet of modules, run them to failure, label the data, train a CNN on current and voltage signatures to classify the fault mode. Add LSTMs on junction-temperature and bond-wire resistance time series for the degradation trajectory. For remaining-useful-life, a hybrid Gaussian-process-plus-neural-net gives you a point prediction and an uncertainty band — which is what a shift planner actually needs. The flywheel: modern IGBT and SiC modules ship with embedded junction-temperature sensors and DC-link telemetry. More sensors means more data means better models. That's the feedback loop that makes this transition from "interesting research" to "deployed standard practice" basically inevitable.

One quick reframe before the hard problems. Section II groups AI not by method but by task — four of them: optimization, classification, regression, and data-structure exploration. The punchline: pick the task first, then the toolkit. "Classify this fault signature" — classification, use CNN or SVM. "Predict days to failure" — regression, use LSTM or a Gaussian process. The failure mode I see in a lot of papers is people starting from a cool method and bending the problem to fit. This slide is the corrective. Small slide, useful rule.

Four open problems — the paper is honest about where this gets hard. One: data scarcity. You can't collect training data for a fault mode that hasn't happened yet, which is exactly the case you most want to detect. Most papers in the survey quietly duck rare-event detection. Two: interpretability. A neural-net controller on a grid-connected inverter has to pass IEC functional-safety certification, and no certification body has a procedure for certifying a black box. This isn't theoretical — it's a commercial blocker shipping today. Three: real-time constraints. The controller runs on a microcontroller with a few MIPS between switching interrupts, not a server GPU. A two-hundred-megabyte transformer is irrelevant if you can't inference in two microseconds. Four: hybrid physics-plus-data. The holy grail — combine first-principles models with data-driven components to get the interpretability of models and the flexibility of learning. The paper flags it. Nobody's solved it yet.

Three things I'd argue this paper misses or under-weights. First — temporal blind spot. The taxonomy is 2021. The foundation-model wave that followed barely registers here. In the last two years we've seen foundation models for time-series forecasting, transformer-based HDL code generation, control-pretrained models — the three-by-four matrix looks genuinely different today, and that matters. Second — sim-to-real gets a paragraph where it deserves a chapter. Most RL and NN controllers in the literature are simulator-trained, and simulators smooth over nonidealities that bite on real silicon. The paper names the problem; it doesn't weight it. Third — and this is my real bet — I'd push back on the seventy-eight / twelve split. Control dominates today because the problem is clean. But the data flywheel in maintenance is structural, and it only gets stronger — every new sensor adds to the corpus. Redo this survey in 2030 and I'll take the bet: maintenance is thirty percent of the field, not twelve.

Three things to leave with. One — AI in power electronics is here. Not speculative, deployed across the full lifecycle. Two — the practical framing is: pick your task first, then your method. Optimization, classification, regression, or data exploration. Then metaheuristics, ML, fuzzy, or expert. Three — the open problems aren't a sign the field is broken. They're the reason it's interesting. Thank you.
Hold for questions. If nothing comes in three seconds, prompt with: happy to go deeper on maintenance — that's the part I'm most bullish about.
